explicit_text: I was born in Brazil in 1984, specifically in Belo Horizonte. In April 1996, my family and I left Brazil for what was supposed to be a temporary trip to New York City. Although my family eventually returned to Brazil, I chose to stay in the United States to pursue my education and build a new life.

explicit_text: When I first arrived in New York City in 1996, I was amazed by the skyline and especially the World Trade Center. The city felt like a new world full of endless opportunities, far beyond anything I had imagined. It was in New York City that I began to see the possibility of making a life here, even though my family planned to return to Brazil.

explicit_text: The United States became my home after I arrived as a child in 1996. Even though my family returned to Brazil, I stayed behind to pursue my education and the American Dream. Despite challenges related to my undocumented status, the U.S. offered me opportunities that I was determined to seize, especially after the introduction of DACA in 2012.

explicit_text: Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals, or DACA, was a turning point in my life when it was announced by President Barack Obama on June 15, 2012. Before DACA, my undocumented status blocked me from many opportunities like getting legal documents and enrolling in college. After applying, I finally received my employment authorization and social security card, which felt like a huge relief and a key to achieving my dreams.

I will always be grateful for the opportunities that came from his leadership during that time.

explicit_text: I am currently a student at the University of Minnesota in Minneapolis. After receiving my legal documents through DACA, I was able to enroll and pursue higher education, which had been impossible before. Studying here has been a vital part of my journey toward achieving the American Dream.

explicit_text: The United States Supreme Court made a landmark decision on June 26, 2013, when it struck down the Defense of Marriage Act (DOMA). This ruling granted same-sex couples equal immigration rights, which was a meaningful development during my time as a DREAMER. The decision reflected progress in civil rights that affected many people, including myself.

explicit_text: The Defense of Marriage Act, or DOMA, was a law that limited immigration rights for same-sex couples until it was struck down by the U.S. Supreme Court in 2013. This change meant that same-sex couples gained equal immigration rights, which was a significant victory for equality. It happened during a time when I was also experiencing important changes in my own immigration status as a DREAMER.
